context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on the City Case Study Investigation spider diagram for my climate action course. I’m not totally sure how to organize my city’s info around the 5 pathways, so could you help me start breaking that down?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—but you need to do the sorting. Let’s start simply: what is your case study city?

Then list the 5 pathways from your course materials, and under each one, jot 1 concrete city action, policy, or problem you’ve found. If you’re stuck, tell me the city and which pathway feels most unclear, and we’ll unpack that one first.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay within the assignment scope and use guided discovery rather than organizing the city for the student. To keep the support minimal and productive, I’ll prompt the student to identify their case study city and begin sorting evidence into the 5 pathways themselves. My questions will move directly toward a workable spider diagram structure without giving content or wording they can submit.

exchanges.student: My case study city is Jakarta. I think I can find examples, but I’m a little fuzzy on the exact 5 pathways from the course materials. Could you help me confirm those first so I don’t sort things into the wrong categories?
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct to verify first. Don’t guess here.

Open the course page or notes and find the exact names of the 5 pathways for urban transformation. Paste them here exactly as your course states them, and then we’ll test Jakarta examples against each one so you don’t mis-sort them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not supply uncertain course-specific content from memory if the student’s materials define it. To avoid giving potentially wrong categories, I’ll direct the student back to the assignment/course page and use questions that help them verify the exact labels themselves. This keeps the student responsible for the work while moving them toward accurate categorization.

exchanges.student: I checked, but I’m still not confident I found the right wording. I think they’re about governance, planning, buildings/infrastructure, energy or resources, and behavior, but I’m probably mixing them up. Can you give me a hint about what I should look for in the exact pathway titles?
exchanges.tutor:
You’re probably in the right neighborhood, but don’t lock in those labels yet.

Look for titles that describe broad levers of urban transformation: one about governance/institutions, one about spatial or land-use form, one about physical systems or infrastructure, one about resource/energy flows, and one about social or behavioral change.

Now check your course page again: which exact 5 titles seem to match those buckets?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will avoid asserting exact course labels from memory since the student needs accurate assignment-specific wording. I’ll use guided discovery by giving high-level cues about how pathway titles are usually framed, then ask the student to verify against the course page. This supports accuracy without doing the student’s retrieval work or giving a submit-ready answer.

exchanges.student: I think I found them, but I want to make sure I’m not still paraphrasing. I have: urban form, urban infrastructure, governance, innovation, and behavior change. Are those the exact ones I should use?
exchanges.tutor:
Those sound very likely to be the right 5. But for submission, use the exact wording from your course page, not memory.

Next step: for Jakarta, which one feels easiest to fill first—governance, infrastructure, urban form, innovation, or behavior change? Give me 1 example you already found, and we’ll test whether it fits that pathway.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should help the student verify alignment without overcommitting to exact wording unless they confirm it from the course materials. I will acknowledge the likely match, remind them to use the course’s exact wording, and move the discussion toward applying the categories to Jakarta through a focused question. This maintains the Socratic method and keeps the student doing the work.

exchanges.student: Governance feels easiest. I found Jakarta’s sea wall and flood-control projects, but now I’m worried that might actually fit infrastructure more than governance. Can you help me tell the difference?
exchanges.tutor:
Good catch—that distinction matters.

Ask yourself: is your evidence mainly about who decides, coordinates, funds, regulates, or enforces? That points to governance. Is it mainly about a built system or physical project? That points to infrastructure.

For the sea wall example, what is the core of your source: the structure itself, or the institutions behind it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will clarify the distinction through contrastive questions rather than categorizing the example for the student. This keeps the student reasoning about what makes an item governance versus infrastructure, directly supporting accurate sorting for the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: I think the core is mostly the structure itself, so that should be infrastructure. For governance, maybe I should use something about Jakarta’s agencies or flood-policy coordination instead, but I’m not sure what counts as specific enough.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—“agencies or coordination” is the right direction, but right now it’s too vague.

For governance, look for one of these: a named agency, a policy or regulation, a coordination body, a funding decision, or a city-national partnership.

What is one concrete Jakarta governance item you’ve actually found in a source?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a useful distinction, so I should reinforce it and push them toward specificity without supplying examples. I’ll ask for concrete governance indicators—agency names, policy instruments, coordination mechanisms—so they can identify suitable evidence themselves. This remains bite-sized and solution-directed.

exchanges.student: I found references to Jakarta’s provincial government working with the national government on flood mitigation and coastal protection. I’m not sure if that’s concrete enough yet, but it seems more governance-related than the sea wall itself.
exchanges.tutor:
Better. It sounds governance-related, but it still needs a sharper anchor.

Ask: which actors, working together on what decision or policy mechanism?
Can you identify 1 named ministry/agency plus 1 specific coordination action—planning, funding, regulation, or implementation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a plausible governance example but it remains vague. I should push them toward a more concrete, source-based formulation without providing wording for the assignment. A focused Socratic prompt about actors, mechanism, and decision will help them test whether the evidence is specific enough for the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: I’m getting stuck on making it specific. I saw the provincial government and national ministries mentioned, but I’m not sure which single coordination action to put in the spider diagram without overexplaining. Could you help me figure out what level of detail is enough for one cell?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. For one cell, “enough” is usually just 3 parts: who, did what, about what issue.

So test your item this way: which actor(s), what coordination action, and for what flood/coastal problem?

Write those 3 pieces in note form here—not polished sentences—and I’ll tell you if it’s specific enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck on scope, so I should reduce the task and define a practical threshold for sufficiency without drafting content for them. I will give a minimal structure for what one spider-diagram cell should contain—actor + action + issue—then ask them to fill it from their source. This is less Socratic because they are circling on the same detail, but it still avoids providing the actual entry.

exchanges.student:
Okay, maybe like this:  
- who: Jakarta provincial government + national public works ministry  
- did what: coordinated flood mitigation planning/funding  
- about what issue: coastal flooding and land subsidence  

I think that’s closer, but I’m still not sure if “planning/funding” is too broad.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s close enough to be useful, but yes—“planning/funding” is still too broad. Pick one.

Ask yourself: does your source emphasize making a plan, paying for a project, or coordinating implementation? Choose the strongest one.

Which single verb is best supported by your source?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has a workable structure. I should give concise evaluative feedback, note the remaining vagueness, and push them to choose one action rather than two. This keeps the guidance minimal and moves them directly toward a usable spider-diagram entry without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: I think “planning” is the strongest one from what I read. So my governance note would be: Jakarta provincial government + national public works ministry; coordinated planning; for coastal flooding and land subsidence. Is that specific enough to use for the spider diagram?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—specific enough for a spider-diagram note, assuming your source really supports those actors and that action.

Don’t overwork one cell. Bigger picture now: pick the next pathway. Which is easier for Jakarta—urban infrastructure or urban form?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a sufficiently concrete note for a spider-diagram cell. I should confirm its adequacy without polishing or rewriting it, and then move them forward to the next pathway to avoid over-fixating on one detail. This follows the instruction to be bite-sized, critical when needed, and to redirect from redundancy toward the bigger picture.
